source: Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act 2016 — RERA
title: RERA — Real estate buyer protections against builder fraud and delayed possession
text: The Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act 2016, commonly known as RERA, protects homebuyers from builder fraud and project delays. Every real estate project must be registered with the state RERA authority before any bookings or sales are made. The builder must disclose all project details including layout plan, approvals, timeline, and carpet area. Under RERA, if a builder fails to deliver possession of the flat or apartment on the promised date, the buyer has the right to withdraw from the project and receive a full refund of the amount paid with interest at the prescribed rate. Alternatively, the buyer can choose to continue with the project and receive interest on delayed possession for every month of delay. A buyer who has been cheated by a builder — through non-delivery, misrepresentation of amenities, or failure to provide promised specifications — can file a complaint with the state RERA Authority. The RERA Authority can direct the builder to refund the money with interest and pay compensation. The RERA Appellate Tribunal hears appeals against RERA Authority orders. Complaints under RERA can be filed online on the respective state's RERA portal. RERA complaints can also be combined with consumer complaints under the Consumer Protection Act 2019 — a buyer can approach both RERA and the Consumer Forum simultaneously for different reliefs. The limitation period for RERA complaints is not expressly defined, but complaints should be filed promptly after the cause of action arises.
